Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/oh-my-pi-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bwbo_ybl...

Analyzing oh-my-pi-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bwbo_ybl) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines two sources: a release binary (`omp-linux-x64`) and the LICENSE file, both fetched over HTTPS from the official GitHub repository of the project. The `sha256sums` are provided and non-SKIP, ensuring integrity verification of the downloaded binaries. The top-level scope contains no executable commands or command substitutions, and there is no `pkgver()` function (so `pkgver` is static), meaning no dynamic version computation occurs during sourcing. The only function defined, `_install_completions`, is only called during the `package()` phase, which is not executed under `--noprepare --nobuild --nodeps`. Therefore, sourcing this PKGBUILD and downloading the sources poses no risk of remote code execution, data exfiltration, or unintended side effects.
</details>
<summary>
No unsafe operations during source fetch</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No unsafe operations during source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, LICENSE-16.1.0...
LLM auditresponse for LICENSE-16.1.0:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is a license file (LICENSE-16.1.0) containing the standard text of the MIT License. It includes copyright notices for Mario Zechner (2025) and Can Bölük (2025–2026), followed by the typical permissions and disclaimers associated with the MIT license. There is no executable code, no network-related commands, no file manipulation instructions, and no obfuscated content.

The content is purely declarative and legal in nature, intended to define the software's licensing terms. It does not perform any actions, request external resources, or modify the system. As such, it poses no security risk. Standard AUR packaging practices expect license files to be included as-is, and this file conforms to that expectation.

This file is not part of the build or installation logic but is likely installed as documentation. Its contents are benign and consistent with open-source licensing norms.
</details>
<summary>
Standard MIT license file, no malicious content.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE-16.1.0. Status: SAFE -- Standard MIT license file, no malicious content.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a pre-built binary (`omp-linux-x64`) and its corresponding LICENSE file from the official GitHub repository and release page of the project. The source URLs are correctly parameterized with the tagged version (`v${pkgver}`), and the binary is installed directly without modification. The integrity of both files is verified using hardcoded, reproducible SHA-256 checksums, which is a secure practice for binary packages.

The `_install_completions` function generates shell completions (bash, zsh, fish) by running the binary with specific subcommands in a controlled environment (`env HOME=... XDG_DATA_HOME=...`). This is a legitimate and safe use of the binary to extract completion scripts, and the operations are confined to the build directory. No network calls, dynamic code generation, or file system modifications outside the package directory occur.

There is no use of `eval`, `curl|bash`, or obfuscated payloads. The package does not pull content at build time from mutable refs (like `git pull`), nor does it execute arbitrary remote code. All actions are consistent with standard AUR packaging practices for a binary release.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary AUR package with proper checksums and safe completion generation
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary AUR package with proper checksums and safe completion generation
Reviewed all required files.
Deciding which files to review next...
Decided to review 1 additional files: .editorconfig
Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing .editorconfig...
LLM auditresponse for .editorconfig:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `.editorconfig` is a standard configuration file used by text editors and IDEs to maintain consistent coding styles across different environments. It specifies formatting rules such as line endings (`end_of_line = lf`), insertion of a final newline (`insert_final_newline = true`), and trimming of trailing whitespace (`trim_trailing_whitespace = true`). These settings are benign and pose no security risk.

There is no executable code, no network operations, no file system manipulations beyond editor behavior, and no obfuscation. The presence of this file in an AUR package is common and expected for maintaining code style consistency during development. It does not affect the build or runtime behavior of the software.

This file is purely ergonomic and does not alter system state, exfiltrate data, or introduce malicious behavior. Its contents are entirely within the scope of normal, safe project metadata.
</details>
<summary>
Editor configuration file, no security issues</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed .editorconfig. Status: SAFE -- Editor configuration file, no security issues
Reviewed all additional files.
Reviewed additional files.
Audit complete! Result: No issues found

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable assignments and function definitions at the top level. No code executes during sourcing that could be malicious. The `source` array points to the official GitHub releases and LICENSE file for the project, with pinned SHA256 checksums. The `_install_completions` function is only called inside `package()`, which is not executed by the requested commands. There is no obfuscation, no dangerous commands (eval, curl|bash, etc.), and no exfiltration or backdoor behavior. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will safely download the declared sources and parse the PKGBUILD without executing any malicious code.
</details>
<summary>No malicious top-level code; safe to fetch sources.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>

<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard binary release package for an AI coding tool. It downloads a pre-compiled binary and license file from the project's official GitHub releases, with pinned version tags and valid SHA-256 checksums (not SKIP).  
The `_install_completions` function runs the installed binary to generate shell completion scripts for bash, zsh, and fish — a common and expected practice for many CLI tools, not a security concern. The environment variables (HOME, XDG_DATA_HOME) are isolated to temporary directories to avoid affecting user data during packaging.  
No obfuscation, dangerous commands (eval, curl|bash, base64), or unexpected network activity is present. The file is consistent with legitimate AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary release; no malicious behavior detected.</summary>
</security_assessment>
